parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces substantial new surface area (retry logic, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, state management) with several material risks. The most concerning is the retry library's interaction with non-idempotent operations and the BigModel error classifier's potential to mask genuine failures by misclassifying them as retryable. Insufficient guards around state file mutations under concurrent access also warrant attention before shipping.

findings.title: Retry logic may re-execute non-idempotent operations causing data duplication or corruption
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs is a new 200+ line retry library. If callers pass functions that mutate state (e.g., job submission, tracked-job updates), automatic retries on transient errors will re-execute side effects. Without an explicit idempotency key or deduplication mechanism in the retry wrapper, any caller that isn't itself idempotent will silently duplicate work. The test file (tests/retry.test.mjs) at 352 lines likely tests the retry mechanics but cannot guarantee callers use it safely. This is especially dangerous for glm-client.mjs calls that submit or transition job state.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site that invokes `retry()` and verify each is idempotent. For non-idempotent callers, add explicit guard documentation or require an idempotency wrapper. Consider adding a required `idempotent: true` opt-in flag to the retry function so non-idempotent calls fail fast rather than silently retrying.

findings.title: BigModel error classifier may misclassify fatal errors as retryable, masking real failures
findings.body: scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 lines) is a new error dispatch module that classifies API errors to decide retry behavior. If the classification logic has false positives (marking auth errors, quota exhaustion, or data corruption errors as retryable), the system will burn through retry budget on unrecoverable failures, delaying user-visible error reporting and potentially making the problem worse (e.g., rate limits). The tests (tests/bigmodel-errors.test.mjs, 189 lines) may not cover edge cases like HTTP 403 with unusual response bodies, 408 with partial write, or 5xx responses that actually indicate permanent schema changes.
findings.recommendation: Verify the error classifier defaults to non-retryable for any unrecognized error pattern (fail-closed). Ensure HTTP 401/403 are never classified as retryable. Add explicit test cases for ambiguous status codes and malformed response bodies to confirm fail-safe behavior.

findings.title: State file mutations lack concurrency protection risking corruption under parallel access
findings.body: scripts/lib/state.mjs (32 lines changed) manages persistent state. If two concurrent invocations of the companion tool (e.g., two terminal sessions or a hook + manual command) write state simultaneously, the last-write-wins behavior can silently drop state updates. No file locking (flock, lockfile, or atomic rename) is visible in the diff. This can cause tracked jobs to disappear or status to report stale data, which is hard to detect and diagnose.
findings.recommendation: Use atomic write (write to temp file + rename) for state file updates. Add an advisory file lock (e.g., proper-lockfile or flock) if concurrent writes are possible. At minimum, document the single-writer assumption clearly if concurrency is out of scope.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs changes introduce new failure paths in API communication with unclear error propagation
findings.body: scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs has 155 lines changed, integrating with the new retry and bigmodel-errors modules. If the retry/backoff logic swallows an error and the caller expects to handle it, the error propagation chain may be broken. Additionally, if the client returns a stale or partial response after exhausting retries without clearly signaling that to the caller, downstream code may operate on incomplete data silently.
findings.recommendation: Verify that after all retries are exhausted, the original error (not a wrapped/generic one) propagates to the caller with full context. Add logging at each retry attempt including attempt number and error details so failures are observable in production. Ensure callers cannot mistake a retry-exhausted error for a successful response.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs changes may leak job tracking state on unclean shutdown
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) manages job lifecycle tracking. If a process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, system crash) between starting a job and recording its completion, the tracked job will remain in a perpetually 'running' state with no cleanup mechanism visible. Over time this accumulates stale entries that pollute status reporting and may block new job submissions if there's an upper limit.
findings.recommendation: Add a stale-job cleanup mechanism (e.g., a TTL or startup sweep that marks jobs as abandoned if they've been running beyond a reasonable timeout). Consider adding a startup health check that reconciles tracked state with actual system state.

next_steps: Read scripts/lib/retry.mjs fully and trace all call sites to verify idempotency
Read scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs and confirm fail-closed default for unrecognized errors
Read scripts/lib/state.mjs and check for atomic write or locking mechanisms
Read scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs error propagation after retry exhaustion
Read scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs for stale job cleanup logic
